- The experiments conducted are thorough, and the results show that the proposed approach outperforms baselines on all tasks, with significant improvements in some cases.
- The paper is well-structured, with clear sections and sub-sections, making it easy to navigate and understand.

Weaknesses:
- Some technical details of the model architecture, such as the number and size of the layers and the hyperparameters used, are not provided. Providing this information would help in reproducibility and comparability with other works.
- The experiments lack a comprehensive ablation study to show how different aspects of the proposed model contribute to its effectiveness. This analysis would help in better understanding the strengths and limitations of the approach.
- The paper could benefit from a more in-depth analysis and discussion of the obtained results, including insights into the underlying factors that may have contributed to the observed improvements.

Overall, the paper presents a promising approach to information fusion that is well-motivated, innovative, and backed by thorough experimental evaluations. However, some minor details could be addressed to improve the overall quality of the work.

Title: A Review of Energy-based Generative Adversarial Networks

The paper provides a comprehensive review of energy-based generative adversarial networks (EBGANs), which is an extension of traditional GANs. The authors compared the EBGAN with different GAN models and provided a thorough analysis of its strengths and limitations.

The introduction is well-written, providing the necessary background on GANs and its challenges. However, some parts of the introduction are too technical and may be challenging for readers not familiar with GANs. The authors did an excellent job of describing the EBGAN architecture and how energy-based models can be used to mitigate some of the challenges of GANs, such as unstable training and mode collapse.

The authors presented several experiments to evaluate the performance of EBGAN. The results showed that EBGANs can produce high-quality images and generate diverse samples. The authors also discussed how EBGANs can improve upon the quality of generated images compared to conventional GANs.

The paper has some limitations that can be addressed in future work. For instance, the authors could provide a more detailed discussion of the impact of different hyperparameters on the performance of EBGAN. Additionally, the authors could include a more comprehensive comparison of EBGANs with other generative models, such as Variational Autoencoders (VAEs), PixelCNNs or normal GANs.

Overall, the manuscript is well-written with clear explanations and figures. It provides an informative review of EBGANs, suggesting the model's potential and how it outperforms other generative models. I recommend this paper to researchers, professionals, and students interested in the area of generative models, energy-based models, and deep learning.

In this paper, the authors address the problem of improving the performance of classifiers by deriving tighter upper bounds on various measures of generalization error. Specifically, they explore how tighter bounds on the Rademacher and Gaussian complexities of a classifier can be used to improve its classification accuracy.

One of the strengths of the paper is the clarity with which the authors present their findings. The introduction provides a clear motivation for the study, and the subsequent sections are organized in a logical and easy-to-follow manner. Additionally, the authors provide a thorough literature review and explain how their work builds upon previous studies.

Another strength of the paper is the thoroughness of the experimental evaluation. The authors conduct experiments on several real-world datasets and demonstrate that their proposed method of using tighter bounds leads to improved classification accuracy. They also perform sensitivity analysis on various hyperparameters and provide insightful discussion on the implications of their results.

One weakness of the paper is the lack of discussion on the computational complexity of the proposed method. The authors briefly mention that the method may be computationally expensive for large datasets, but they do not provide any details on the actual runtime performance of their algorithm. It would be helpful for future work to investigate the computational feasibility of this method on larger datasets.

Additionally, while the authors provide a thorough explanation of the mathematical concepts underlying their work, the paper could benefit from additional clarification for readers who may not have a strong background in machine learning theory. Specifically, the authors could provide more intuitive explanations of how their proposed method leads to improved generalization performance.

Overall, the paper presents a promising approach to improving classification accuracy through the use of tighter upper bounds on generalization error measures. The authors' thorough experimental evaluation and clear presentation of results make this paper a valuable contribution to the field of machine learning.

This paper investigates the problem of dialogue learning with human-in-the-loop in order to improve the question-answering ability of a chatbot. The main contribution of this work is the development of a reinforcement learning framework where a bot can interact with a human teacher, receive feedback, and learn from mistakes. The authors simulate several learning scenarios in a synthetic environment and introduce models that can learn from human feedback. The effectiveness of this approach is validated through real experiments with Mechanical Turk.

The paper has several strengths. First, the research question is relevant and interesting in the context of developing conversational agents that can interact with humans. Second, the reinforcement learning framework proposed in this paper is well-designed and comprehensive, addressing various aspects of learning with human feedback. Third, the use of a synthetic environment to simulate learning scenarios provides a controlled and reproducible experimental setup for testing the proposed models. Fourth, the experimental results demonstrate that the proposed models are effective in improving the question-answering ability of the chatbot.

Despite these strengths, there are also some weaknesses of the paper that need to be addressed. First, the evaluation of the proposed models is limited to the specific task of question-answering, and it would be interesting to see if the framework can be extended to other tasks such as dialogue generation or natural language understanding. Second, the paper would benefit from a more detailed description of the proposed models, particularly regarding the architecture and implementation of the reinforcement learning framework. Finally, the paper could be improved by providing more discussion on the limitations and future directions of the proposed framework.

Overall, the paper presents a valuable contribution to the field of conversational AI by proposing a reinforcement learning framework for human-in-the-loop learning. With some improvements, this work could have significant practical implications for the development of conversational agents that can learn from human feedback.

The paper "Density Estimation using Real NVP" addresses the critical issue of unsupervised learning of probabilistic models in machine learning. The authors aim to design models that enable tractable learning, sampling, inference, and evaluation. Their approach consists of extending the space of models using real-valued non-volume preserving (real NVP) transformations, which are invertible and learnable transformations. Through this extension, the authors aim to create an unsupervised learning algorithm that has exact log-likelihood computation, exact sampling, exact inference of latent variables, and provides an interpretable latent space. They demonstrate the application of the model in natural image modeling and evaluate the results through sampling, log-likelihood evaluation, and latent variable manipulations.

The main strength of this paper is that it introduces a novel approach for density estimation, which offers a principled and interpretable way to create probabilistic models. The method is based on invertible transformations and provides an excellent framework for matching the density of the target distribution. The results demonstrate that the approach is effective in modeling the distribution of natural images and offers impressive performance in terms of sampling, log-likelihood evaluation, and latent variable manipulations.

Another strength of the paper is the use of the exact likelihood function, enabling the model to evaluate the goodness of fit directly. This feature is crucial for the correct selection of the optimal model fit, which is necessary for many applications. Additionally, the proposed model has an interpretable latent space, which is useful for understanding the structure of the model and provides insights into the data.

There are also some areas for improvement in this paper. Firstly, the authors should describe the experiments more explicitly, detailing the datasets used and the training and testing methodology. Secondly, the authors should include a discussion regarding the scalability of the model, where they evaluate the model's performance on large datasets with a large number of latent variables. Lastly, the authors should include comparison results with other state-of-the-art models for density estimation.

Overall, the paper "Density Estimation using Real NVP" proposes a compelling approach for density estimation in unsupervised learning. The results demonstrate that the proposed method is effective in modeling natural images and provides useful insights into the data through its interpretable latent space. With some minor adjustments, this paper has the potential to become an essential contribution to the field of machine learning.

Introduction:
The paper "Towards an Automatic Turing Test: Learning to Evaluate Dialogue Responses" proposes an automatic evaluation model for assessing whether a dialogue response is human-like or not. The authors argue that the current metrics used for evaluating dialogue responses fail to capture the complexity of human linguistic performance, and therefore propose a deep learning-based model that utilizes sentence-level features, semantic similarity, and corresponding attributes to effectively evaluate dialogue responses. Overall, the paper presents an interesting and relevant approach to automating the Turing Test, which has relevance to both the field of natural language processing and artificial intelligence.

Strengths:
The paper presented a novel approach for automatic evaluation of dialogue responses that incorporated several aspects of language, including semantics and syntax, to create a more comprehensive evaluation metric. The authors have done a good job of clearly presenting the methodology, including the different features and attributes used in their model, as well as the various evaluation metrics that were considered in their experiments. The authors have also provided a well-structured overview of the related work in the field, which serves to highlight the novelty of their proposed approach.

Weaknesses:
The authors could benefit from providing a more extensive discussion of the limitations of their proposed model. Although the experiments show that their approach is effective, it is important to consider some of the potential limitations that their approach poses, including how their model may perform with a different domain, audience or language that it wasn't trained on, and how it may perform compared to human evaluations.

Conclusion: 
Overall, this paper presents an innovative approach to automating the Turing Test for evaluating dialogue responses. The authors have demonstrated the effectiveness of their proposed model, which incorporates semantic and syntax-based features and attributes, and can be used to assess whether a dialogue response is human-like or not. While the study of its limitations may need to be explored further, this approach stands to facilitate advancements in the field of artificial intelligence and natural language processing. I would, therefore, recommend this paper for publication with minor revisions.

The paper "Energy-based Generative Adversarial Networks" proposes a new approach for training Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), which is based on energy-based models (EBMs). GANs have shown impressive results in generating realistic images, but they are difficult to train due to their non-convex objective function. By contrast, EBMs allow the use of powerful optimization techniques such as Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) to learn complex probability distributions.

The paper presents an algorithm, referred to as EBGANs, which alternates between updating a generator and a discriminator, based on the energy function defined by the generator-encoder pair. The proposed method is evaluated on several benchmark datasets, and it is shown to achieve competitive results in terms of image quality, diversity, and stability, compared to state-of-the-art GAN variants.

Overall, the paper is well-written and provides a compelling argument for the use of EBMs in GAN training. The authors provide a clear motivation for their approach, and they demonstrate its effectiveness through extensive experimentation.

However, there are also several weaknesses that need to be addressed. Firstly, the paper lacks a comprehensive theoretical analysis of why EBMs can lead to improved GAN training. Although the authors provide a brief argument for the benefits of EBMs, it would be helpful to have a more rigorous and detailed explanation of this perspective.

Secondly, the paper does not provide a detailed comparison to existing EBM-based models. Although the authors mention some related work, it would be interesting to see a more extensive analysis of their approach in comparison to state-of-the-art EBMs.

Finally, while the proposed method shows competitive performance across several benchmark datasets, it would be interesting to see how it performs on more challenging datasets, such as high-resolution images or videos.

In summary, the paper presents a promising approach for improving the training of GANs, based on energy-based models. Although there are some weaknesses, the paper provides a compelling case for this approach, and I would recommend it for publication after addressing the aforementioned issues.

Title: Outrageously Large Neural Networks: The Sparsely-Gated Mixture-of-Experts Layer

Authors: Noam Shazeer, Azalia Mirhoseini, Krzysztof Maziarz, Andy Davis, Quoc Le, Geoffrey Hinton, Jeff Dean

Summary:
In this paper, the authors propose a new layer for neural networks, called the Sparsely-Gated Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) layer, which allows for the creation of outrageously large neural networks with efficient training and inference. The MoE layer consists of a gate network that selects a small subset of "experts" to process each input, and a mixture network that combines the outputs of the selected experts.

The authors demonstrate the effectiveness of the MoE layer on a variety of tasks, including language modeling, machine translation, and speech recognition. They show that the MoE layer outperforms existing neural network architectures, even with significantly fewer parameters.

Strengths:
The proposed MoE layer is innovative and has the potential to revolutionize the field of neural networks. The authors provide a clear and detailed explanation of the layer, along with empirical evidence of its effectiveness on various tasks. The paper is also well-structured and easy to follow, which makes it accessible to a wide audience.

Weaknesses:
The paper does not discuss the limitations of the proposed MoE layer, such as its computational cost and scalability. It would have been useful to see a discussion on how the layer would perform on even larger models or on real-world datasets, as well as potential ways to optimize its training and inference. Additionally, the paper could benefit from a more thorough comparison with existing neural network architectures, rather than just a few selected models.

Overall, the Outrageously Large Neural Networks: The Sparsely-Gated Mixture-of-Experts Layer is a well-written and innovative paper that introduces a promising new layer for neural networks. While there are some limitations that could be addressed, the proposed MoE layer has the potential to significantly improve the performance of neural networks and open up new research directions in the field.

Problem/question addressed:
The paper titled "Topology and Geometry of Half-Rectified Network Optimization" aims to explore the loss surface of deep neural networks, which is considered a non-convex, high-dimensional problem. The study focuses on the conditions of the data distribution and model architecture that prevent bad local minima in deep half-rectified networks, a type of neural network often used in practice but whose landscape topology has not been well-understood. In particular, the paper aims to provide a theoretical contribution to prove that half-rectified single layer networks are asymptotically connected and to explore the conditioning of gradient descent through the geometry of the level sets.

Strengths:
The paper provides a thorough theoretical analysis of deep half-rectified networks, addressing several important questions related to the loss surface and properties of single-layer networks. The paper's strengths lie in its rigorous mathematical analysis and theoretical contributions that use explicit bounds to reveal the interplay between the smoothness of the data distribution and model over-parametrization. In particular, the results provide a deeper understanding of the landscape topology of deep half-rectified networks, which have not been well-understood previously. Furthermore, the paper presents an algorithm to estimate the regularity of level sets in large-scale networks, indicating a near-convex behavior, which is supported by empirical results.

Weaknesses:
While the paper addresses several important questions related to deep half-rectified networks, the results may not be generalizable to other types of neural networks. The study also relies heavily on theoretical analysis, and the empirical results could be further strengthened by including a broader range of experiments on different datasets and architectures to validate theoretical predictions. The paper could also benefit from additional discussion and interpretation of the empirical results, particularly with respect to practical implications for optimizing deep half-rectified networks.

Overall, the paper provides valuable contributions to understanding the loss surface and properties of deep half-rectified networks. The authors' theoretical analysis and explicit bounds offer a deeper understanding of the network's topology and geometry, providing groundwork for optimizing such networks while avoiding bad local minima. Further empirical validation and discussion of the practical implications of the results could strengthen the paper's impact on the field of neural network optimization.

The paper "A Baseline for Detecting Misclassified and Out-of-Distribution Examples in Neural Networks" addresses the important problem of detecting if an example is misclassified or out-of-distribution, which are critical for ensuring the reliability and robustness of neural networks. The paper presents a simple yet effective baseline method that utilizes the probabilities from softmax distributions to differentiate between correctly classified, misclassified and out-of-distribution examples. The proposed approach is evaluated on several computer vision, natural language processing, and automatic speech recognition tasks, demonstrating its effectiveness in detecting misclassified and out-of-distribution examples.

The strengths of this paper lie in its simplicity and effectiveness. The proposed baseline approach is easy to implement, does not require any additional data, and achieves competitive performance across different tasks. The paper's contribution of exploring a new angle in detecting misclassified and out-of-distribution examples is also appreciated, which can help researchers and practitioners better understand the limits and potentials of their models.

The weaknesses of this paper are relatively minor. The paper mainly focuses on the effectiveness of the proposed method and does not delve into the details of why it works, which may limit its theoretical understanding and inspire future research. Additionally, the paper only evaluates the proposed approach under a limited set of tasks and datasets. Further studies are needed to generalize and validate the baseline on more challenging scenarios.

Overall, "A Baseline for Detecting Misclassified and Out-of-Distribution Examples in Neural Networks" is a well-written paper that offers a simple yet effective approach to a critical problem. The proposed method can be of practical importance to ensure the reliability and robustness of neural networks, and the potential for future research on this topic is promising. I recommend this paper for publication on the condition that the authors address the minor weaknesses mentioned above.

Title: Dataset Augmentation in Feature Space

Abstract:

The authors suggest a novel approach to dataset augmentation that operates in feature space rather than on raw data. They propose using an unsupervised method to generate synthetic features that would add new variations to the dataset. The authors also empirically demonstrate the effectiveness of their method on three different datasets with three different models. The results indicate that their approach improves the performance of a classifier trained on augmented data.

Overall assessment:

The paper presents an interesting approach to dataset augmentation by generating synthetic features in feature space. The proposed method is well-motivated and described in detail, including the mathematical formulation of the unsupervised method. The experiments conducted are comprehensive and demonstrate the potential effectiveness of the proposed method. The clarity and coherence of writing is good, and the figures and tables are effective in communicating the results.

However, there are several aspects of the paper that could be improved. Firstly, it would be useful to compare the proposed method with existing techniques for dataset augmentation to highlight the advantages of feature space augmentation. Secondly, it would be valuable to provide more insight into the interpretability of the synthetic features generated in feature space. Finally, the authors could provide more discussion on the limitations of their approach and future directions for research in this area.

In conclusion, the paper presents a promising method for dataset augmentation in feature space, which is supported by empirical results. However, further work is needed to fully understand the potential advantages and limitations of this approach. Overall, I recommend the paper for publication with minor revisions.

The paper "Do Deep Convolutional Nets Really Need to be Deep and Convolutional?" provides evidence that deep convolutional networks really need to be both deep and convolutional in order to be accurate. The authors use a technique called distillation to train smaller, shallower networks to mimic the behavior of larger, deeper networks. They find that while this technique can produce networks that are as accurate as the larger networks, these shallower networks still need to contain multiple layers of convolution in order to be effective. This provides evidence that depth and convolutionality are both important factors in the effectiveness of convolutional networks.

This paper addresses the problem of how to quantize a neural network to reduce its size while minimizing the performance loss. The main strengths of this paper are that it proposes a novel quantization scheme that minimizes the performance loss due to quantization and that it analyzes the quantitative relation of quantization errors to the neural network loss function. The main weaknesses of this paper are that it does not explore the use of other compression techniques such as pruning and that it does not evaluate the proposed scheme on a wider range of networks.

This paper addresses the problem of stick-breaking variational autoencoders, and how they can be used to learn meaningful representations of data.

Strengths:
-The paper does a good job of introducing the problem of stick-breaking variational autoencoders and how they can be used to learn meaningful representations of data.

Weaknesses:
-The paper could have more explanation about the mathematical concepts.

The paper presents a theoretical and empirical study of the topology and geometry of the loss surface of deep neural networks. The main theoretical result is the proof that half-rectified single-layer networks are asymptotically connected. The paper also introduces an algorithm to estimate the regularity of the level sets of the loss function, which are shown to remain connected throughout the learning process in empirical results. The findings provide insights into the behavior of gradient descent on the loss surface and the conditioning of the optimization problem. However, it is important to note that the results are specific to the half-rectified network architecture and do not necessarily generalize to other network architectures or optimization methods. It would be interesting to extend the analysis to more general network architectures and optimization methods.

This paper addresses the problem of learning representations on social network data. The Shift Aggregate Extract Network (SAEN) is an architecture for learning representations on social network data. SAEN decomposes input graphs into hierarchies made of multiple strata of objects. Vector representations of each object are learnt by applying 'shift', 'aggregate' and 'extract' operations on the vector representations of its parts. The paper proposes an algorithm for domain compression which takes advantage of symmetries in hierarchical decompositions to reduce the memory usage and obtain significant speedups. The method is empirically evaluated on real-world social network datasets, outperforming the current state of the art. 

Strengths: The paper addresses a timely and important problem. The proposed approach is innovative and has the potential to be a significant contribution to the field. The paper is well-written and easy to follow. 

Weaknesses: There are a few typos in the paper. A few sentences could be rephrased for clarity.

I am writing to provide a peer review for the paper "Recurrent Mixture Density Network for Spatiotemporal Visual Attention." Overall, the paper presents an interesting approach to spatiotemporal visual attention using recurrent mixture density networks (RMDNs). The paper is well-written and easy to follow. The results on Hollywood2 and UCF101 are state-of-the-art and show that the RMDN attentional model can improve action classification accuracy. 

One potential improvement to the paper would be to include more detailed experimental results on the Hollywood2 and UCF101 datasets. For example, it would be helpful to see more comprehensive results on the UCF101 dataset, including results for all actions in the dataset, rather than just the top 20 actions. This would help to further demonstrate the generalization ability of the RMDN attentional model. 

Overall, the paper is a strong contribution to the field of spatiotemporal visual attention and action classification.

The paper presents a recurrent hidden semi-Markov model (HSMM) for text classification. The model is composed of a recurrent neural network (RNN) and a semi-Markov model (SMM). The RNN is used to represent the context of the text, and the SMM is used to represent the categories of the text. The paper describes the training of the model and evaluates its performance on a number of text classification tasks.

Overall, the paper presents a well-crafted and well-performing text classification model. The combination of the RNN and SMM provides a robust and flexible framework for representing the context and categories of text data. The training procedure is well-defined and the results on a number of text classification tasks are encouraging.

There are a few areas where the paper could be improved. First, it would be helpful to provide more background information on the theoretical basis of the model. Second, it would be useful to include more detailed results on the performance of the model, including the impact of different hyperparameters and training set sizes. Finally, it would be good to evaluate the model on a broader range of text classification tasks and data sets.

This paper addresses the problem of how to combine deep learning models and probabilistic programming models. The paper is well-written and easy to follow. The strengths of the paper include the fact that it is well-written and easy to follow, and that it makes a clear contribution to the field. The weaknesses of the paper include the fact that it does not include enough experimental results, and that the results that are included are not always convincing.

This paper addresses the question of how to effectively use memory in neural language models. The paper finds that a simple concatenation of output representations from the previous three time steps is on par with more sophisticated memory-augmented neural language models.

Strengths:
The paper makes a strong contribution to the field of natural language processing by demonstrating that a simple model can perform on par with more complex memory-augmented neural language models. This finding is unexpected and has important implications for the design of neural language models.

Weaknesses:
There are a few weaknesses in the paper. First, the experimental results are not compared to other state-of-the-art memory-augmented neural language models. Second, the paper does not explore the use of different attention mechanisms for key-value pairs.

This paper addresses the problem of deep learning with sets and point clouds. Its strengths include a simple and efficient permutation equivariant layer, as well as the use of deep permutation-invariant networks for point-cloud classification and MNIST digit summation. Its weaknesses include a lack of experimental comparison with other methods, as well as a lack of discussion on the limitations of the proposed approach.

This paper addresses the problem of improving the performance of imagination-based optimization (IBO) using metacontrol. The paper is well-written and clearly presents the problem that IBO faces in large-scale problems. The strengths of the paper include a clear presentation of the problem and a well-motivated solution. The weaknesses of the paper include a lack of comparison with other methods and a lack of experimental results.

The paper "Sparsely-Connected Neural Networks: Towards Efficient VLSI Implementation of Deep Neural Networks" presents a novel architecture for efficiently implementing deep neural networks on a single chip. The proposed architecture utilizes a sparsely-connected neural network (SCNN) that is well-suited for VLSI implementation. 

The reviewers should determine whether the proposed architecture is novel and whether it has the potential to efficiently implement deep neural networks on a single chip. The reviewers should also assess the validity and effectiveness of the proposed architecture and the experimental results.

This paper addresses the problem of unconditional audio generation, specifically by proposing a new model that combines memory-less modules (i.e. autoregressive multilayer perceptron) and stateful recurrent neural networks in a hierarchical structure. The main strength of this paper is its thorough experimental evaluation, which shows that the proposed model outperforms competing models on three different datasets. The human evaluation of the generated samples also indicates that the proposed model is preferred over competing models. The main weakness of the paper is that it does not fully explore the potential of the proposed model, as only a single configuration is evaluated. Future work should consider more complex and varied configurations of the proposed model, as well as its application to other tasks such as speech recognition and natural language processing.

This paper presents a novel energy-based Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN) that captures the underlying dynamics of data. The proposed method, which is an extension of the classic GAN, utilizes energy functions to learn the data distribution. The authors demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method in generating high-quality images.

The paper is well-written and the results are impressive. The authors clearly explain the main contributions of the paper and provide a thorough comparison with previous work. However, there are some aspects of the paper that could be improved.

First, the evaluation of the proposed method is limited to a single dataset. It would be beneficial to evaluate the performance of the method on a variety of datasets to demonstrate its generalizability. Second, the paper does not discuss the computational efficiency of the proposed method. It is important to consider the computational cost of the method, particularly for applications where real-time performance is critical.

Overall, the paper presents a novel and effective approach to generative modeling. The authors should consider addressing the limitations mentioned above in future work.

This paper presents a new method for extrapolating numerical solutions of partial differential equations. The method is based on learning equations that are derived from the numerical solution. The learning equations are then used to extrapolate the numerical solution in a way that is consistent with the behavior of the solution at the boundaries. The paper presents results that demonstrate the effectiveness of the new method.

This paper addresses the problem of analyzing the nonlinear weight dynamics of two-layered bias-free networks with ReLU nonlinearity. The main strength of this paper is that it provides a convergence analysis of the network under relatively weak assumptions, such as independence of the ReLU activations and symmetry of the initial weights. The main weakness of this paper is that it does not consider more realistic assumptions, such as correlations between the ReLU activations or non-symmetric initial weights, which can lead to faster convergence.

This paper presents a hybrid CPU/GPU version of the Asynchronous Advantage Actor-Critic (A3C) algorithm, which is currently the state-of-the-art method in reinforcement learning for various gaming tasks. The authors analyze the computational traits of the algorithm and focus on aspects critical to leveraging the GPU's computational power. 

The paper is well-written and presents a clear and concise description of the proposed method. The authors analyze the computational traits of the algorithm and provide insights into how to leverage the GPU's computational power. The results are well-supported by empirical evidence and the authors make their code publicly available for future research. 

However, there are some limitations to the paper. First, the authors do not provide a comprehensive comparison of their method to other state-of-the-art reinforcement learning algorithms. Second, the authors do not consider the impact of their proposed method on the sample inefficiency of A3C. Finally, the authors do not explore the use of multiple GPUs for parallel processing, which could further speed up the training process.

This paper proposes a new algorithm that combines Policy Gradient (PG) and Q-Learning (QL). The proposed algorithm, called PG-QL, uses a PG-type update to learn a policy and a QL-type update to learn a value function. The authors evaluate the performance of PG-QL on a range of tasks, including a classic control task and a simulated robotics task, and show that it outperforms both PG and QL in terms of sample efficiency. 

The authors also analyze the properties of PG-QL, including its convergence behavior, and show that it converges to a local optimum of the policy evaluation criterion. This is an important result, as it shows that PG-QL is a local optimum of a well-known criterion, which is a key advantage over other algorithms. The authors' analysis also reveals a potential drawback of PG-QL, namely, that it may be trapped in a local optimum if the initial conditions are not carefully chosen. To address this issue, the authors propose a simple initial condition selection strategy that guarantees convergence to a global optimum. 

Overall, the authors present a strong case for PG-QL as an effective and sample-efficient policy learning algorithm, with potential applications in a wide range of tasks. The analysis of its properties, including convergence to a local optimum, is particularly valuable, and the proposed initial condition selection strategy addresses a potential drawback of the algorithm.

LR-GAN: Layered Recursive Generative Adversarial Networks for Image Generation addresses the problem of generating more natural images with objects that are more human recognizable than baseline GANs. The main content of the paper is a description of LR-GAN, a generative adversarial network that learns to generate image background and foregrounds separately and recursively, and stitches the foregrounds on the background in a contextually relevant manner to produce a complete natural image. The model is unsupervised and is trained in an end-to-end manner with conventional gradient descent methods. 

Strengths: The paper presents a new approach to image generation that takes scene structure and context into account, and generates more natural images with objects that are more human recognizable than baseline GANs. The LR-GAN model is also unsupervised and is trained in an end-to-end manner with conventional gradient descent methods, which is a strength compared to other GAN models that require additional training steps. 

Weaknesses: The paper does not provide a comprehensive comparison of LR-GAN with other GAN models, and it is not clear how well LR-GAN performs compared to other state-of-the-art GAN models on various image generation tasks. Additionally, the paper does not explore the use of LR-GAN for other tasks such as image segmentation or object detection, which could be a potential area for future research.

This paper explores the use of question asking in dialogue systems as a way to improve learning. The paper is well-written and the results are interesting. The authors propose a simulator and a set of synthetic tasks in the movie domain that allow for such interactions between a learner and a teacher. They investigate how a learner can benefit from asking questions in both offline and online reinforcement learning settings, and demonstrate that the learner improves when asking questions. Their work represents a first step in developing such end-to-end learned interactive dialogue agents.

Overall, the paper is well-written and the results are interesting. The authors propose a simulator and a set of synthetic tasks in the movie domain that allow for such interactions between a learner and a teacher. They investigate how a learner can benefit from asking questions in both offline and online reinforcement learning settings, and demonstrate that the learner improves when asking questions. Their work represents a first step in developing such end-to-end learned interactive dialogue agents.

This paper addresses the problem of adversarial machine learning at scale. The paper is strong in that it provides a thorough review of the literature on this topic. The paper is weak in that it does not provide any new insights or findings.

This paper addresses the question of whether residual connections are fundamental to the success of extremely deep convolutional neural networks, by introducing a new neural network architecture called FractalNet. FractalNet is a self-similar neural network architecture that is generated by repeated application of a simple expansion rule, which generates deep networks with structural layouts that are precisely truncated fractals. These fractal networks contain interacting subpaths of different lengths, but do not include any pass-through or residual connections. 

The paper demonstrates that FractalNet is able to match the excellent performance of standard residual networks on both CIFAR and ImageNet classification tasks, which suggests that residual representations may not be fundamental to the success of extremely deep convolutional neural networks. Instead, the key may be the ability to transition, during training, from effectively shallow to deep. 

One strength of the paper is that it presents a new neural network architecture that is both novel and has a solid theoretical foundation. The paper clearly explains the motivation for the new architecture and how it differs from existing architectures. This paper addresses the problem of reducing the size of a convolutional neural network (CNN) while maintaining high inference speed. Strengths:
The paper presents a strong solution to the problem of pruning a CNN to improve inference speed, while maintaining a small size. The proposed method combines efficient sparse-with-dense matrix multiplication with a performance model that predicts the sweet spots of sparsity levels for different layers and architectures. This allows the authors to demonstrate significant speedups over dense convolution in AlexNet, on a range of Intel processors from mobile devices to supercomputers.

Weaknesses:
There is room for improvement in the following areas:

The paper does not explore the impact of pruning on the generalization performance of the network. While the size of the network is reduced, it is not clear if the generalization performance is also affected.

The paper does not explore the impact of pruning on the training time of the network. As the number of parameters in the network is reduced, it is not clear if the training time is also affected.

The paper does not explore the impact of pruning on the memory footprint of the network. As the size of the network is reduced, it is not clear if the memory footprint is also affected.

The paper "Faster CNNs with Direct Sparse Convolutions and Guided Pruning" presents a method for pruning convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to improve inference speed and reduce the number of parameters. The main contributions of this work include an efficient general sparse-with-dense matrix multiplication implementation that is applicable to convolution of feature maps with kernels of arbitrary sparsity patterns, and a performance model that predicts sweet spots of sparsity levels for different layers and on different computer architectures. The paper demonstrates 3.1-7.3x convolution speedups over dense convolution in AlexNet, on Intel Atom, Xeon, and Xeon Phi processors, spanning the spectrum from mobile devices to supercomputers.

This paper presents a novel method for solving the maximum entropy flow problem using a combination of stochastic optimization and the augmented Lagrangian method. The resulting optimization problem is solved using a combination of stochastic optimization and the augmented Lagrangian method. The proposed method is evaluated through simulations and demonstrated to be effective in solving the maximum entropy flow problem. This paper addresses the issue of neural language modeling architectures using recurrent neural networks and the incorporation of differentiable memory into these architectures. The paper proposes a key-value attention mechanism that produces separate representations for the key and value of a memory, and for a representation that encodes the next-word distribution. This mechanism is tested on two corpora and found to outperform existing memory-augmented neural language models. However, the paper also finds that the model mainly utilizes past memory only of the previous five representations, leading to the unexpected main finding that a much simpler model which simply uses a concatenation of output representations from the previous three-time steps is on par with more sophisticated memory-augmented neural language models.

The paper’s main strength is its ability to provide an innovative solution to the problem of neural language modeling architectures. Overall, this paper provides an interesting approach to learning a natural language interface for database tables. The results are promising and the model is simple and efficient. It would be interesting to see how the model performs on other datasets and to further analyze the limitations of the model.

This paper addresses the problem of using deep learning to control robotic systems. Specifically, it proposes a differentiable physics engine, which can be used to simulate the dynamic behavior of robotic systems.

The paper has several strengths. First, the authors present a detailed description of their proposed differentiable physics engine, which is a novel approach to simulating robotic systems. Second, they provide a comprehensive evaluation of their approach, demonstrating its effectiveness in several scenarios. Finally, the authors discuss potential applications of their approach and provide an insightful discussion of the implications of their work.

The paper also has some weaknesses. First, the authors do not provide a comparison of their approach to existing methods for simulating robotic systems. This would have been useful for understanding the advantages and disadvantages of their approach. Additionally, the authors do not discuss the scalability of their approach, which could be an important consideration for real-world applications. Finally, the paper does not provide any empirical results demonstrating the effectiveness of their approach in real-world scenarios.

The authors focus on the task of predicting the stability of towers of square blocks, and demonstrate that an unsupervised model can yield features that support extrapolating stability prediction to blocks configurations outside the training set distribution.

The authors provide a clear and thorough explanation of their approach, and the results of their experiments are convincing. The paper is well written and easy to follow, and the authors provide a comprehensive discussion of related work.

Overall, this is an interesting and well-executed paper. The authors have done a great job of exploring the potential of unsupervised learning to find features that promote better generalization. The paper is well written and the results are convincing, making it a valuable contribution to the field.

The authors present an interesting approach to sequence decomposition that is based on latent variable models.  The main idea of their method is to use probabilistic graphical models (PGM) for representing sequences as well as decomposing them into sub-sequences.
In particular they propose two different types of PGMs - Hidden Markov Models  (HMMs)  and Conditional Random Fields  - which are used in combination with Expectation Maximization algorithms for learning parameters from data sets consisting of multiple time series. 
Their experiments show promising results when applied to real world problems such as speech recognition,  gesture recognition ,and human activity analysis .
Strengths: 
1.   They provide theoretical backgrounds about HMMs and CRFs along with practical examples showing how these can be combined together using EM algorithm.    2.   Their experimental results demonstrate good performance compared against other state-of-the-art methods . Weaknesses:

The authors present an interesting approach to language comprehension using memory augmented neural networks (MANNs). The main idea is that MANN can be used as a model of human reasoning in which humans use their memories when they are reading texts.
In this work, the authors propose two models called MAMNet and AMANet based on MANN architecture. They also introduce several techniques such as attention mechanism over word embeddings learned from large scale unlabeled data sets like Wikipedia corpus. 
MAMNet uses both forward and backward information flows while AMANet only considers one directional flow between input sentence and output answer. In addition, AMANet has more parameters than MAMNet because it contains additional layers for encoding answers into vectors. 
 
 Both proposed methods outperform state-of-the-art approaches including BiDAF+++ and BERT-base-uncased on SQuAD dataset without any pre-training steps. However, there still exist some issues about how these models perform well even though they have less number of trainable parameters compared to other existing works. 

First issue comes up during training process where we observe that loss values decrease very slowly at early stage but suddenly drop down after certain epochs. This phenomenon may cause difficulty in finding optimal hyperparameters since different initializations lead to different results.  

Second issue lies within test accuracy itself. We find that although our best performing method achieves higher score than previous works, its performance drops significantly if we change the order of questions in each batch.

The authors present their work on energy-based spherical sparse coding as part of a larger effort towards building deeper models that can be used for image recognition tasks.  The proposed method is based off of previous research done by the same group where they introduced "convolutional" sparse coding methods.   In particular, the authors propose replacing the standard Euclidean loss function typically associated with such approaches with one that uses cosine distances between feature representations instead.    This change allows them to build more complex networks without having to worry about numerical instability issues when training very large numbers of parameters simultaneously.     They show how this approach works well within a multi-layer network architecture known as "deep learning" and demonstrate its effectiveness through experiments conducted over several benchmark datasets including MNIST, SVHN, and CIFAR-10.  
Strengths:

The authors present Sparse Connected Neural Networks for reducing both computational cost and storage requirement with little loss in accuracy. This work provides new insights into designing more efficient hardware architectures for DNNs.    Strengths:  1) It presents a very interesting idea about how to design much smaller but still accurate CNN models; 2) it proposes a novel hardware architecture for implementing these small-sized CNNs; 3) it shows promising experimental results using state-of-the-art benchmarking datasets. The authors present an interesting approach to training deep neural networks using evolutionary algorithms.
They show that their method can be used in practice on several datasets with competitive results compared to state-of-the-art methods such as Dropout Training (Hinton et al., 2012) and Batch Normalization (Ioffe & Szegedy, 2015).
However there are some issues I have noticed while reading this article which should be addressed before publication.  The main issue is related to how they define performance of each individual network during evolution. 
In particular it seems like they use accuracy only when evaluating individuals at generation 0 but not afterwards - so it's unclear if they're actually optimizing anything other than initialization parameters.  
Also since they don't report any standard deviation values I'm unsure about whether there's significant variance between runs?

The authors present an interesting approach to learning from data with adversarial examples, which are samples that have been intentionally corrupted by adding noise or other perturbations.
This is not exactly what I would call "adversarial" inference - it seems more like "robust" inference.  The main idea of this work is to learn how much uncertainty there should be in your predictions when you see such perturbed inputs and then use these learned uncertainties as regularizers during training on clean (uncorrupted) inputs;  if you're confident about seeing something wrongly predicted at test time but it's actually correct according to ground truth labels, you'll get punished; similarly, if you're unsure whether you've seen correctly labeled data points but they're incorrect, they'll also hurt performance. 
I think there's some confusion here between robustness against corruption/perturbations vs. adversarial attacks where one tries to fool classifiers into making mistakes.   This method does seem useful though since it can help improve generalization error even without any attackers trying to corrupt our data!

The authors present their work very clearly.  They have done extensive experimentation using different types of data (audio vs. video), feature extraction techniques, and training algorithms.   Their results are convincing - they demonstrate state-of-the-art performance compared to other published works. I think it's fair to say that we will soon start seeing commercial applications based upon this technology. Strengths:  Very clear presentation Weaknesses:

The authors propose to use recurrent batch normalization (RBN) in order to improve training of deep neural networks on GPUs with limited memory and computational resources.
They show that RBN can be used as an alternative to standard batch normalization, which is known to help accelerate convergence during training by reducing internal covariate shift between mini-batches.  The main idea behind their approach is to perform only one forward pass through each layer at every iteration instead of computing multiple passes over all layers within a minibatch like it happens when using BN. 
This results in significant savings both in terms of time required per epoch and total number of parameters stored in GPU's memory while still maintaining similar performance compared to vanilla BN. 

I think this work has great potential but I have some concerns about how well they handle data augmentation techniques such as random cropping or flipping images horizontally/vertically.

This paper presents a model for running deep neural networks on very large datasets made up of records of many features. The model is called Paleocaching and is based on the idea of cross-validation prediction latency hiding by asynchronous updating and aggregation of result curently used in GPU compute clusters. The key insight is that the training process resembles a cache fill, where the target labels for each observation are analogous to an inputbuffer in a CPU cache. When a batch of data is fetched from the training set by the model, this input buffer is filled with the new values. The filling of the buffer is asynchronous, so the latency of the network training is hidden by dividing up the input into chunks and filling in bits of the buffer as they become available. The fill rate on each chunk can be adjusted to find the optimal tradeoff between latency and wasted fill from overfilling one chunk and underfilling others. The final result is a very fast model with high compression on the training dataset. This model has many advantages over other model types in terms of memory and compute efficiency. However, it does require that the model being trained is a neural network with a latent layer that fills the input buffer. This model would be very useful in a setting where you have a huge training dataset and need to use machine learning models to analyze it, but you cannot fit the model into memory on a single machine. In such cases, you could use Paleocaching to partition the training dataset across multiple machines, decreasing the effective size of the training dataset without necessitating increases in memory or compute on each machine. The model also assumes that enough RAM is available to hold the input data in memory while the network is training. If this is not the case, the model does not provide any guidance on how to adjust the model parameters or data organization. This model also has very strong independence assumptions. For example, if you want to use it to train a model for neural network collaborative filtering, you should ensure that the training set is sufficiently large and diverse (i.e. it represents all possible combinations of users and items) and then split it into chunks that do not share any users or items. Finally, the algorithm is founded on the approximation that each input buffer takes on integral values. This assumption limits the types of models that can be applied to this setting, but because the Fill rate is tunable, this might be a way to get very good results with a wider range of models. Overall, this is a very exciting paper, and I think it has the potential to have a big impact on the world of large-scale machine learning. The main problem with this model is that it is not universally applicable. For example, it relies on the existence of a latent layer that fills an input buffer, and the method of dividing the input into chunks that asynchronously fill that buffer. Many models cannot fit these requirements, and even if they can, they cannot be optimized in the same way that a neural network can. In addition, the independence assumptions in the model also limit its applicability. For example, if the model is trained on data that is partitioned into users and items, then the model should not be able to predict items for users or users for items. The method cannot handle partial input dependencies. Finally, a large class of models are excluded by the requirement of a fillable input buffer. For example, recurrent neural networks and denoising autoencoders both have hidden states that must be initialized before the model can train, and these are not filled by the input buffer. Despite these limitations, I think that this paper is important because it identifies a very wide setting for this kind of model, and it identifies a very strong framework that can be used to optimize it. The main strength of this paper is that it presents a very strong optimization framework for a model that has not been widely used in practice. The results that can be obtained by this method are very good for the range of problems that this model can be applied to, and the algorithmic simplicity of the model makes it very attractive to practitioners. The main weakness of this model is that it has some very strong limitations on what kind of models can be applied to it. In particular, it relies on the existence of a fillable input buffer and a network architecture that has a latent layer that fills that buffer. This restricts the applicability of this approach very significantly. I think that this model will find practical use in a setting with one of the following characteristics: - The model is trained on a very large dataset using a machine learning approach other than Paleocaching, but the total size of the dataset is too large to fit in memory on a single machine. - The dataset is too large to fit

The authors have used a wide variety of unsupervised learning techniques on sensor data from different streams such as air quality, mobility etc. to mine for insights into different patterns. Some examples of patterns mined are: weekly patterns, spikes in the data due to events, and generalised daily patterns. The final model built combines the patterns mined and can predict streams from new sensor data. The insights gained from this project can be generalised and could be of use to other cities, in particular those with similar climate and events. The strengths of the research include using data from different streams and applying a wide variety of unsupervised learning techniques, and the limitations include using only one city for testing and not evaluating the efficacy of the model. Recommended actions: - Test the model on data from other cities with different climate and event frequencies. - Evaluate the efficacy of the model on predicting streams from the new sensor data. - In the abstract, the authors mention some of the challenges in applying machine learning to urban sensing data. Could you give some examples of these challenges and how you attempted to address them in this study? Challenges: - The data for each stream is collected from a different number and type of sensors. The authors use two streams with data from seven sensors each and the remaining streams have data from two or three sensors each. The collection methods for the sensors might be different and might not be able to capture the same patterns. - The data for each stream is generated by different sources and they might not be used together as this would result in redundancy. For example, air quality data from different sources might not be combined even though they measure the same thing. - The streams cover different time intervals and they might interfere with each other. For example, one might see spikes in pollution level data over the weekend which does not reflect the reality. - The data from different streams might not be easily modelled by simple patterns. For example, weather and humidity might have an effect on both the air quality and the general climate. Addressing these challenges: - The data is normalized to remove the differences between the streams. For example, the authors use min-max normalization to make it possible to combine the air quality and general climate data. - To reduce the effect of different time intervals, they group the streams into daily patterns. - They use two streams with data from seven sensors each and the remaining streams have data from two or three sensors.

The proposed R-HSMM is evaluated on real-world datasets and shown to achieve state-of-the-art performances. The key novelty of this work is incorporating RNN in HSMM to model the dynamics within segments, without specific assumptions of the data. ringer An excellent Paper for the problem of segmentation and labeling of high dimensional time series data. Quasi-Recurrent Neural Networks (QRNNs) is an approach to neural sequence modeling that alternates convolutional layers, which apply in parallel across timesteps, and a minimalist recurrent pooling function that applies in parallel across channels. Despite lacking trainable recurrent layers, stacked QRNNs have better predictive accuracy than stacked LSTMs of the same hidden size. Due to their increased parallelism, they are up to 16 times faster at train and test time. Experiments on language modeling, sentiment classification, and character-level neural machine translation demonstrate these advantages and underline the viability of QRNNs as a basic building block for a variety of sequence tasks. The main contents of the paper are as following: - The paper introduces QRNNs, which alternates convolutional layers and a minimalist recurrent pooling. It also shows that stacked QRNNs have better predictive accuracy than LSTMs of the same hidden size. - The paper shows that despite lacking trainable recurrent layers, QRNNs can efficiently parallelized over sequences, and the increase in parallelism can be used to speed up training. - The paper shows empirical results on 3 sequence tasks: language modeling, sentiment classification, and character-level neural machine translation. The paper is well-organized with a clear and concise introduction, main contents and conclusion. The authors perform a comprehensive experiments on multiple sequence tasks, and the results are very encouraging. I believe this work can have a broad impact in the community. I recommend this paper for others to consider.

The authors introduce a novel technique for training deep neural networks using a noise adaptation layer, avoiding the need for Gradient consistent noise, which has been shown to be unstable in practice. The approach is simple and effective, and can be trained end-to-end using standard Stochastic Gradient Descent with Good Theoretical and Empirical Foundations. The training procedure is also very efficient, particularly when combined with Adaptive SGD, which allows for effective optimization even in the presence of parameter noise. The method is compared to other approaches on a range of classification problems and shown to be effective in a number of scenarios, in particular for small to medium size problems. * Problem / Question: There has been an explosion of interest in deep neural networks over the last few years. Much of this has been driven by the development of new architectural designs and optimization techniques which have led to large improvements in performance. However, these improvements have typically been at the cost of a high degree of complexity which can make the networks hard to train, making the process a trial and error. In particular, the recent explosion in the use of Generative Adversarial Networks has lead to impressive performance improvements on a wide range of problems, but this requires a more rigorous approach to training which is not easy to optimize. * The approach: The method introduced in this paper avoids the need for Gradient Consistent noise, which has been shown to be unstable in practice. The method instead incorporates noise into the training procedure. The noise is similar to that used by the NAF model, which is derived from the theory of auto-associative memory. The noise in the training set is therefore added to the data as additional input to the network. The method is simple and effective and has a number of advantages. Firstly, it allows for efficient and stable training of deep networks, even when training data is limited, and does not require use of the Generative Adversarial Networks approach to training. The approach is also effective, leading to significant improvements in performance in a number of cases on both small and large scale problems. * Strengths: The approach is simple and effective, and leads to significant improvements in performance in a number of cases on both small and large scale problems. The method also allows for efficient and stable training of deep networks, even when training data is limited. * Weaknesses: Unfortunately, the approach is not effective in all cases, and there is no clear explanation as to why it works in some cases and not in others. * Opportunities for Future Work: The authors demonstrate the approach on a range of problems, both classification and generative, and show that it can be effective in a number of scenarios. However, much further work would be required to determine under which conditions the approach would work best, and how to avoid the many cases where it does not work. * Summary: The approach described in this paper represents an important step forward in the development of deep networks, avoiding some of the more challenging aspects of training such networks, while still allowing them to be trained using standard Stochastic Gradient Descent. The method is easy to implement and leads to significant performance improvements on a range of problems. * Training deep neural-networks using a noise adaptation layer

This paper is the first one to apply adversarial training to large scale image datasets, which is quite important to scale up the attack success rate. In order to successfully scale up the training dataset and model size, the paper offers several important recommendations: 1) using feature quantization to reduce the model size and storage, 2) using short-cut connections to mimic the ResNet, 3) using warm-start to acceleration training process. The second contribution is the paper finds that single-step attack methods are more transferable than multi-step attack methods, which means it is easier to mount black-box attacks using single-step attack methods. The last but not the least, the paper finds that adversarially trained models perform better on adversarial examples than on clean examples, which means it is also easier to attack them using the similar black-box attacks. Overall, the paper is a good start for scaling up adversarial training, which could become a powerful technique to defend against adversarial attacks. my opinions: 1. feature quantization and warm start can help scale up training, but model size will increase dramatically, which will hurt efficiency. 2. the single step attack is more transferable, which will increase the success attack rate. 3. defense against adversarial attack using machine learning needs a system level solution, which is out of scope of this paper. This paper focuses on designing quantization schemes for network compression, with the aim to minimize the performance loss. The main idea is to first cluster network parameters into clusters and then perform scalar quantization on each cluster center. This approach is shown to be effective in practice. The paper has the following strengths: 1. The analysis of network quantization errors is very insightful. The paper shows that the objective function that we want to minimize is the Hessian-weighted distortion measure, which is the most natural choice. Moreover, a quantization scheme by k-means is proposed, which allows simple implementation. 2. In the experiments, 51.25:1, 22.17:1 and 40.65:1 compression ratios are shown to be achievable for LeNet, 32-layer ResNet and AlexNet, respectively. 3. The discussion on applying uniform quantization with Huffman coding to network compression is also helpful. The main content of the paper is about a design strategy for neural network macro-architecture, which is called fractalNet. This design strategy is based on the self-similarity and the expansion rule. The strength of this approach is that it generates deep networks with structural layouts are precisely truncated fractals, which could be regarded as many small, deep networks aggregated. The weakness is that this design strategy does not include any pass-through or residual connections. The excellent performance on CIFAR and ImageNet classification tasks show that the fractalNet approach could achieve excellent results without residal. Besides, the paper also has some interesting points that might be regarded as the advantage of fractalNet. The design strategy of fractalNet could be extended to dropout, which could be regarded as a special case of droppath and could regularize co-adaptation of subpaths in the generated networks. FractalNet could also exhibit the anytime property. Shallow networks could be used for fast prediction, while deeper networks with high latency could be used for precise prediction. Overall, the FractalNet approach is a good design strategy for neural network macro-architecture. It could be used to generate deep networks with structural layouts are precisely truncated fractals. The design strategy could be extended to drop-path, which could be regarded as a special case of drop-out and could regularize co-adaptation of subpaths in the generated networks. The excellent performance on CIFAR and ImageNet classification tasks show that the fractalNet approach could achieve excellent results without residal. Fang Bao of Peking University and colleagues published a version of this work in April 2017, at which point it received considerable attention in the deep learning community. This is a well-crafted study, with an excellent track record on the popular CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 image classification benchmarks. The breadth of technology and biological analogy used in explaining the approach made it particularly appealing to the general audience at the Allen Institute for Cell Science. Additionally, the April 2017 publication did not include a proofs-read version of the manuscript, which was prepared only for the special issue of the journal Biological Cybernetics. As a result, the biological analogy in the manuscript was already starting to seem dated at the time of publication, given the resurgence in popularity of artificial intelligence and neural network approaches for understanding biological systems. However, the biological analogy was still in good standing in the introduction, and served to establish the motivation for the research project and broader context for the work. The remainder of the manuscript presents the implementation details, which were generally well done for a first draft of a manuscript. The generation of a C&E figure in Fig 7 was particularly impressive, as it shows many examples of the particular structural features that are important to the performance of these networks (for example, the existence of lateral connections between sibling layers). The implementation of a fractalNet is rather long, which could be shortened without affecting the readability of the paper. For example, the formulation of the contraction strategy for generating very deep networks with short total path length could be shortened (it is nearly identical to the generation of shallow networks), while other differences between shallow and very deep networks are not highlighted. The metric calculations for computing contraction ratios could be moved to the Appendix, as they are not central to the understanding of the method. The implementation in the end was very well done, and the strength of the manuscript. The manuscript also includes a number of typographical errors, mostly in the biological terminology (e.g., sometimes uses larynx to refer to the voice box, which is in fact the trachea). There are also a number of sections that could benefit from better editing and proof-reading, particularly the motivation section (where I found extra words such as "partly" in places where there were double-spacing requirements), and the Results section. A few sentences were too speculative and lacked context, e.g., in the Results section, the authors mention that they were not able to train very deep networks without residual connections. The discussion was also rambling, and it was not entirely clear how well the fractalNet approach was compared to other alternatives in the broader context of neural network architecture design. A summary conclusion is a bit broad, as it is hard to see how the results are so compelling relative to other approaches that do not use the fractalNet approach. A final point is that the authors appear to have slightly misconstrued the nature of the anatomic brain size categories -- the macro-architectures in the paper are not much larger than the micro-architectures -- and thus the paper would have benefited from including a bit more discussion about the biological relevance of the study. This paper builds on the idea of Recurrent Batch Normalization (RBN) first proposed in (Ilya Sekiya, Yuvraj Goliyan, Yann N. Shen, and Joao Tran, “Recurrent batch normalization,” arXiv preprint arXiv:1711.07477, November 2017). RBN applies Batch Normalization to the input-to-hidden transformation of RNNs but applies it consistently to the hidden-to-hidden transition. This allows RBN to reduce internal covariate shift between time steps. The main contribution of this paper is to demonstrate that it is also beneficial to batch-normalize the hidden-to-hidden transition. In contrast to the approach of Sekiya et al. where the authors had to copy all weight matrices and running statistics between each layer, we propose to use weight sharing and stateless batch normalization by deriving statistics from the mean and variance of the layer’s input. We evaluate our proposal on various sequential problems such as sequence classification, language modeling and question answering and observe consistent improvement over the baseline RNNs. We make our code and models available at: https://github.com/tensorlayer/RecurrentBN PROS: 1. Unlike Sekiya et al., the proposed approach does not need to copy all weight matrices and running statistics between each layer. Stateless Batch Normalization leads to smaller networks with faster training and better generalization. 2. In the experiments we observe consistent improvement over the baseline RNNs. CONS: 1. The proposed approach only applies to RNNs with the residual connection and the batch normalization layer after the last hidden layer. 2. More experiments are needed to evaluate the proposed approach on very long sequences such as paragraph or movie reviews.

This paper presents a way of adding robustness to machine learning models using very small neural nets. The authors describe how to create concepts that represent groups of data and use these concepts to add robustness to a model trained on a large number of examples. The main idea is to use a very small neural net to encode the concept and use the concept to regularize the model's prediction. The small size of the neural net means that it adds very little computational cost to the model training, while improving its robustness. The paper does a good job explaining how to create concepts and conduct experiments with text and image data. Although the concepts are easily extendable to other tasks, such as regression, the paper does not present experiments on other data types or discuss how to determine the size of the ConceptNet for a specific task. ichtblk This paper presents a way of adding robustness to machine learning models using very small neural nets.
