This paper addresses the problem of learning continuous semantic representations of symbolic expressions, for more details please see the main content as below. The neural equivalence networks proposed in this work is a step in incorporating continuous and syntax-directed semantic reasoning into a broader framework of program induction. The main idea is to learn deep representations that capture semantic equivalence even of expressions that are syntactically very different. As a strong challenge towards this goal, the expressiveness of the underlying symbolic representation must be carefully managed. Semantic representations must be computed in a syntax-directed manner, because semantics is compositional, but at the same time, small changes in syntax can lead to very large changes in semantics, which can be difficult for continuous neural architectures. We perform an exhaustive evaluation on the task of checking equivalence on a highly diverse class of symbolic algebraic and boolean expression types, showing that our model significantly outperforms existing architectures. The proposed model is motivated in part by recent progress in structured neural networks, where several authors have introduced strategies for sharing computation in deep neural nets via sharing of lower-level compute entities such as op codes, intermediate representations, and data formats. This work has shown that useful computation can be carried out efficiently at run time by reusing pre-computed structures. We adapt this strategy to symbolic expressions, using a highly parameterized equation editor as the low-level compute entity, and sharing a set of neural equivalence network modules to compute on top of it. A key challenge of the approach is that semantic equivalence must be computed in a syntax-directed manner, because semantics is compositional, but at the same time, small changes in syntax can lead to very large changes in semantics, which can be difficult for continuous neural architectures. To address this, we introduce a dynamic composition strategy, in which we decompose a large expression into its component parts, compute a deep representation of each part, and then combine the parts in a concise but semantically expressive way. We perform an exhaustive evaluation on the task of checking equivalence on a highly diverse class of symbolic algebraic and boolean expression types, showing that our model significantly outperforms existing architectures. Overall, this is a step in the right direction towards incorporating continuous and syntax-directed semantic reasoning into a broader framework of program induction. The framework of program induction aims to learn a mapping from observations of examples of program execution to program parameters, and has recently shown impressive results for several classes of functions that are continuous or describe smooth curves or surfaces. However, these programs typically handle a very limited amount of data. Our work shows that improving upon this is crucial to achieve more general and flexible representation and reasoning ability. This work was presented at NeurIPS 18. Strengths: The proposed model is very large model that is capable of representing and reasoning on arbitrary symbolic expressions, including high-level algebra such as program composition and automatic differentiation. As a major advance over prior work, it represents the full extent of the symbolic expression in memory, not just a surface approximation. The proposed approach provides the basis for both exact inference, by choosing a formula in a metal to prove or disprove, and sampling-based learning. The model is trained by minimizing the energy function, which is efficiently computed using dynamic programming on a parse tree. The approach is highly scalable. As a major advance over prior work, it is able to represent and reason on arbitrary symbolic expressions of any complexity, not just very short expressions. The authors show that their model significantly outperforms existing approaches on a large evaluation task involving a diverse set of symbolic algebraic and boolean expression types. Weaknesses: As a major advance over prior work, the model is able to represent and reason on arbitrary symbolic expressions. It is not currently designed to be expressive enough to model certain types of symbolic manipulation, such as simplification, argument substitution, or encoding complex logical rules or properties. The architecture relies on a small number of templates, each representing a fixed set of operations, and it is not clear how to generalize the approach to support a large number of different operators. The approach is not easily adapted to other types of symbolic representation, such as algebraic or XML representation, or to languages other than symbolic algebra and boolean logic. The evaluation is based on a highly restricted set of algebraic and boolean expressions, which limits the ability of the approach to handle more complex types of expression. System Integration System integration of the semantic equivalent operator is a future work, this can be done in the following way. Currently, we can represent this operator as a black box, i.e., it takes as input a semantic representation of an expression and produces as output a semantic representation of the same expression. If we can generate code from a high-level representation of an expression to a low-level representation, we can implement an operator that takes as input a high-level representation of an expression

In this paper, the authors propose Dynamic Coattention Network (DCN) for question answering. The DCN fuses co-dependent representations of question and document to focus on relevant parts of both. Then a dynamic pointer decoder iterates over potential answer spans. This paper makes a meaningful attempt to address the problem that previous deep learning based question answering models are single pass and have no way to recover from local maxima corresponding to incorrect answers. I think this paper is potentially useful for the community and could be published in <INSERT JOURNAL HERE>. My comments are as follows: The authors claim that their model “is the first single-pass deep learning model that can recover from initial local maxima corresponding to incorrect answers”. However, similar claim has been made before (e.g. Xiong et al., 2016). I don’t know if the DCN model is different in any meaningful way. I have read the abstract, introduction and related work, but couldn’t see the critical difference. As far as I can tell, they are equivalent. This paper builds on top of previous deep learning based question answering models. They say that “The DCN model fuses co-dependent representations of question and document“. However, there are lots of papers that also use a similar idea for different models. For example, (Zhu et al., 2017, Xiong et al., 2016) all used a similar idea, namely “co-dependent representations”, to achieve similar tasks. What makes their approach different is the dynamic pointer decoder, which I don’t think has been proposed before. I think a simpler adaptation of a previous model would have the same effect. The paper also compares their approach with previous state of the art. However, there are already several such methods in previous state of the art. For example, DANs, AAN and A3C are all single pass deep learning models for question answering. The comparison might be valid if the previous state of the art also has a single pass model, but I don’t think that is the case.

This paper extends adversarial and virtual adversarial training to the text domain by applying perturbations to the word embeddings in a recurrent neural network rather than to the original input itself. The proposed method achieves state of the art results on multiple benchmark semi-supervised and purely supervised tasks. We provide visualizations and analysis showing that the learned word embeddings have improved in quality and that while training, the model is less prone to overfitting. We note that the paper only considers the semi-supervised text classification task and that the extension to other tasks is worth exploring. Further, we note that the proposed method is not without its computational cost; in particular, to do adversarial training we need to generate many small perturbations to the inputs, which can be expensive. Lastly, we think the method could be improved by using different types of perturbations or by combining with techniques such as small angle approximation, which makes the perturbation distortion small in some sense. Overall, this is an exciting area for future research and we think this paper makes an important first step in that direction. We wonder whether the extension to other tasks is worth exploring. Indeed, we think this is an interesting and worthwhile direction for future work. We also think this paper made an important first step in that direction. Specifically, we think this work: - Is very strong on the text classification task, where it achieves state of the art results on multiple benchmark datasets - Is theoretically interesting in that it first applies adversarial training to the semi-supervised setting - Has a clear modeling choice in that it applies perturbations to word embeddings in a recurrent neural network rather than to the input vectors themselves We wonder if the work can be improved by: - Using different types of perturbations - Using a combination of different training techniques such as small angle approximation - Applying the method to other tasks, for example, the ImageNet classification task We think these are worthwhile directions for future work. Finally, we note that the paper only considers the semi-supervised text classification task and that the extension to other tasks is worth exploring. In this paper, the author addresses the problem of how to accelerate the training of neural networks by efficiently approximating the first order gradient of the loss function with respect to the weight initialization. The technique they propose is quite clever. It decomposes the learning process into multiple small learning steps that are easier to compute. For each small learning step, it estimates the weight update using an adjoint formula. In particular, it solves an optimization problem whose objective function is the loss, and the variables are the weights. This approach is surprisingly effective. When it is applied to deep learning models, it can often reduce the number of effective iterations by a large factor. Moreover, it usually converges very fast. As a result, the training can be speeded up significantly. In the paper, the authors provide several effective techniques to implement this adjoint-based optimization. The last section of the paper is a discussion on the implications and future work. The main strength of this paper is the clever idea of learning weight updates using an adjoint formula. For untrained readers, a key supporting proof in the paper is too long to be included here. But the technique of learning weight updates using an adjoint formula has never been published before. It has a high likelihood of being patented, so the authors chose to post it online instead. The paper also has some weaknesses. First, the technique seems to only be applicable to a certain type of models. For example, it doesn't work well for regularized models with $\ell_p$ norm regularization, where $p>1$. Also, the technique does not work if the loss function is not differentiable. Despite these weaknesses, the paper is very interesting and the techniques seem very promising. I believe this paper will inspire many researchers to try new ways to apply adjoint methods in the field of machine learning. Here is a more detailed discussion on this paper. The paper Introspection: Accelerating Neural Network Training By Learning Weight Evolution introduces a novel approach that uses an adjoint method to compute the first order derivative of the loss function w.r.t. the model parameters. Let us denote the loss function as L(θ, x; y) where θ are the model parameters and x is the training sample. The paper first reduces the learning process into small learning steps. For each small learning step, it estimates the weight update using an adjoint formula which is a function of the loss function L(θ, x; y) and the current weight initialization θ. More specifically, the weight initialization is optimized using an optimization problem with an objective function of the loss function L(θ, x; y) and the constraint of the parameter norm. The idea is quite clever because each small learning step can be efficiently computed because it's a function of the loss function and the current weight initialization. One can approximate the whole optimization by solving the optimization for each small learning step. When applying this to deep learning models, it usually reduces the number of effective iterations by a large factor. Moreover, it usually converges very fast because the algorithm will converge to the optimal weight initialization which can't be violated by the small weight updates. As a result, the training can be speeded up significantly. The paper also has some weaknesses. First, the technique only applies to a certain type of models, i.e. models with first order gradients. So it doesn't work for regularized models with L2 or L1 regularization, where p > 1, or models without first order gradients. Despite these weaknesses, the paper is very interesting and provides a promising direction for weight initialization in deep learning models. This may spark some interests for other techniques for weight initialization. Paper discusses how to build a model that can be trained to transform human-like symbolic programming instructions into low-level machine code. The main challenge in training such a model is finding similar programming instruction pairs that differ in their machine code. The authors use a two-stage training approach. In the first stage, a look-up table model is trained to convert programming instructions into numeric representation. In the second stage, this numeric representation is used to generate a set of similar and different synthetic examples. The final model is a mixture of the two models. The main strengths of this approach are that it can be trained from scratch to solve this problem and that it can generate meaningful numeric code for certain high-level programming tasks. The main weakness is that the generated code only makes semantic sense to the model, and it does not produce code that a human could actually write. Mann et al. describe a two-stage training approach to convert human-like symbolic programming instructions into low-level machine code. The first stage uses a lookup table to convert human-like symbolic programming instructions into numeric representation. In the second stage, a deep convolutional neural network is trained to convert the numeric representation into low-level machine code. The final model is a mixture of the two models. The model is able to generate low-level machine code that makes semantic sense to the model but not human-like code. Strength of the numeric code conversion approach is that the model can be trained from scratch to solve this problem. Weakness is that the model generates low-level machine code that makes semantic sense to the model but not human-like code. Overall, this approach can be trained to solve the problem of converting human-like symbolic programming instructions to low-level machine code. Weakness of the model is that generated low-level machine code make sense to the model but not human-like code. Overall, this approach can be considered as an extension of previously published work in this area. References * Mann, S., Ghodsi, A., & Hadsell, J. (2019). These approaches to learning to write code answer a very important problem. This paper aims to address the challenges of reward function engineering and sample-efficiency in imitation learning with deep reinforcement learning (RL). In existing imitation learning methods, reward function engineering is a time-consuming process that requires a system designer to apply human knowledge to infer the mapping from demonstrations to objectives. InRL, this process is even more challenging due to the intractability of the direct numerical optimization of the objective function. To address this, the paper unifies imitation learning and reinforcement learning (IL-RL) by treating the problem of learning a mapping from demonstrations to rewards as an RL problem. The paper then proposes a method that can rapidly infer high-quality reward functions from a small number of demonstrations.The key idea is to use features in a pre-trained deep model to identify the steps of a task, and use the identified steps to learn a reward function. This method does not require any sub-goal supervision.The paper presents qualitative and quantitative results to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. The paper also presents a robot version of the door-opening task with learned reward function, trained with only a video of a human performing the task. Write a peer review by first describing what problem or question this paper addresses, then strengths and weaknesses, for the paper Fine-grained Analysis of Sentence Embeddings Using Auxiliary Predictition Tasks, its main content is as below: There is a lot of research interest in encoding variable length sentences into fixed length vectors, in a way that preserves the sentence meanings. Two common methods include representations based on averaging word vectors, and representations based on the hidden states of recurrent neural networks such as LSTMs. The sentence vectors are used as features for subsequent machine learning tasks or for pre-training in the context of deep learning. However, not much is known about the properties that are encoded in these sentence representations and about the language information they capture. We propose a framework that facilitates better understanding of the encoded representations. We define prediction tasks around isolated aspects of sentence structure (namely sentence length, word content, and word order), and score representations by the ability to train a classifier to solve each prediction task when using the representation as input. We demonstrate the potential contribution of the approach by analyzing different sentence representation mechanisms. The analysis sheds light on the relative strengths of different sentence embedding methods with respect to these low level prediction tasks, and on the effect of the encoded vector’s dimensionality on the resulting representations. Strengths: The proposed method evaluates sentence embedding on several prediction tasks in an exhaustive manner. This could be very useful in better understanding how various sentence representation models perform at different levels of granularity. In particular, in the same setting, some models may assign higher weights to some specific linguistic properties (for example, using the sentence length as an input for representation learning), while other models do not assign any weights to this property. Identifying which models assign higher weights to which linguistic properties is of interest for model improvements. It is noted that this evaluation is hard to achieve using existing large scale datasets which contain one specific prediction task. We also analyze the relation between sentence embedding dimensionality and its performance. It is shown that reducing the sentence embedding dimensionality improves the performance on some prediction tasks but also reduces the performance on others. It is not clear whether and how much these improvements affect the overall performance, relative to other factors that influence the overall performance. Weaknesses: The proposed method requires computing the prediction on a given sentence by using the embedded representation of that sentence as input. It is known that using a pre-trained model to compute the prediction on new data would introduce a huge adaptation bias. A more direct way of evaluating the models using the prediction tasks, such as distantly supervised training, might be more objective. The proposed method analyzes a limited number of prediction tasks, for example, the content-based word similarity and machine translation accuracy. It would be interesting to analyze other prediction tasks such as textual entailment or sentiment analysis. Overall, the paper presents an important direction of analyzing the quality of sentence embedding models and offers a potential method to guide the model improvement by assigning importance to different linguistic properties. The paper Fine-grained Analysis of Sentence Embeddings also discusses how the textual entailment judgement can be formulated as a classification problem to evaluate sentence embedding. Problem: Textual entailment is formulated as a classification problem to evaluate the quality of sentence embedding model. It is not clear that how to use the prediction results of textual entailment as guidance for improving the quality of the model. Solution: Using prediction of textual entailment as guidance to improve the quality of the model is one direction. In addition to predicting the textual entailment using sentence embedding, the paper also analyzes other prediction tasks (such as the word similarity). By combining these prediction tasks, we can guide the model improvement more effectively. In summary, this paper discusses the problem of evaluating the quality of sentence embedding model in a fine-grained level, and presents a way to guide the model improvement by combining multiple prediction tasks. First, I believe the authors have presented a very interesting idea. Autoencoders have been around for decades and this idea of using adversarial learning to generate features is very novel. There are a few points I believe could be further explored and maybe even validated with more experimental data. 1) Can the autoencoder also be used as a generator? i.e. can the encoded features be turned back into images? If so, this could be very powerful and general purpose feature extraction tool. The authors may want to explore this option and also compare it to the adversarially learned features. 2) Did the authors try other optimizers other than SGD? Some recent work has shown that Adam and other first order methods may outperform SGD when used with the cross-entropy loss. 3) The authors mentioned in the paper that they did not use Batch Normalization. I am not sure if this is because of computational constraints or if Batch Normalization would hurt the stability of the training. Again, the latter might be an interesting avenue to explore. Overall I think the work is promising and an exciting new direction for feature learning. I hope the authors continue to explore this promising direction. Overall recommended rating: Noteworthy Drawbacks: None Author(s) Response Thank you for the detailed feedback. We have addressed all the points mentioned in the feedback. The autoencoder now not only can generate images, but also can generate images of the same size as the input. We have also experimented with other optimizers, and SGD is the best choice in our setting. The code and the generated images are now available in the github repo (https://github.com/yunjey/Adversarial-Feature-Learning). Also, we have included a discussion on Batch Normalization in the new version of the paper. We hope the comments will help improve the paper. Kind regards. Problem: We are often interested in understanding how a machine learning model makes a prediction. The typical approach is to show the model predictions along with the target labels. However, this doesn't always provide sufficient context to understand how the model made the prediction. For example, it's difficult to tell whether the model predicts that the patient has the disease because the patient displayed all of the symptoms or simply because the patient has some of the risk factors for the disease. Similarly, it's difficult to tell whether the model predicts that the patient does not have the disease because the patient doesn't display all of the symptoms or because the patient doesn't have all of the protective factors for the disease. second, the proposed method only work for image classification problems. it cannot be applied to other types of data, such as text, time series, or speech. For example, it may be difficult to determine what factors influence the prediction made by the model for these types of data. Third, the prediction difference analysis requires the model to provide a prediction along with a potentially large number of class counts. In some cases, the model may provide a confidence score for each prediction and the prediction difference analysis cannot be performed unless both the prediction and the confidence score are provided. Strength: This paper presents a new approach to visualize how a deep neural network model makes a prediction. The proposed approach, called prediction difference analysis, provides a heat map view of prediction differences between the model predictions and the target labels. The visualization allows the user to quickly understand how the model makes the prediction, including which patient characteristics and image features the model uses to make the prediction. The authors also provide several example use cases for this visualization, such as how to use the prediction difference analysis to evaluate the model's confidence in its prediction, identify problem features in the data that the model has overfit, and identify valid predictions the model made that have high class imbalance. Weakness: The authors only consider prediction difference analysis for deep neural networks that have a single output. In particular, the authors only show the relative change in a target value for a single class in a multi-class classification task. The authors consider a target value of a specific class and the change in the target value for each class in the model's output space (the output space is defined as the range of values that the model predicts for a specific input). However, the relative change doesn't provide the complete picture, because it doesn't provide information on which specific target value the model changed. For example, the authors propose to look at the prediction difference between two inputs. One input is the input that the model should predict for, and the other input is the input that the model actually predicts for. In this case, the authors should provide the change in the target value of each class for the input the model should predict for, and the change in the target value of each class for the input the model actually predicts for. Moreover, the authors only focus on identifying problem features in the data, and not on what specific features the model uses to make the prediction.

The paper "Neural Functional Programming" by Toshiko Hasaka et al. describes a method to convert an arbitrary computation expressed in the Symbolic Deep Learning Language (SDL) into a sequence of operations on numeric tensors, and back. The resulting "neural program" is expressed in terms of the popular TensorFlow framework for Machine Learning. The paper focuses on converting functions of numeric parameters into sequences of operations on numeric tensors. To illustrate the method, several numeric examples are given, including both common models and more advanced treatments that are not easily expressible in other formats. The strengths of the paper include: 1) a clear explanation of the ideas, 2) relatively advanced content for a paper published in a special issue of ICL, and 3) clear and concise examples using both basic models and more advanced treatments. The weaknesses include: 1) no discussion of how to perform experiments to validate the models, 2) no discussion of how to perform experiments to validate the training of the models, and 3) no discussion of more general classes of models beyond the specific examples given. The potential applicability of the method is limited by the same serious weaknesses found in all machine learning: lack of clear experiments and validation, no discussion of actual experiments, and no discussion of more general classes of models. If researchers can define their models in a simple, clear way and perform clear, targeted experiments, this method has the potential to increase the quality of machine learning models and their validation. But to achieve this potential, the paper needs to go beyond explaining the ideas and examples and actually discuss the ideas and how they can be applied to validate models and perform experiments. Overall, the paper has good ideas that could be developed into a more useful paper with careful attention to the kinds of experiments and validation every researcher needs to perform to achieve their goals.

The author has used Real NVP, a neural net library for Torch, to train a Density Estimator. The code is well written and clearly explains the concepts. The topic is already quite challenging due to the author's choice of a hard problem domain (high-dimensional statistics), but the author has written an exceptionally clear and straightforward article. The author could use some professional copy editing and a goodlew spaces. I highly recommend this article for publication after the required editing. Culture & Song of Real NVP Breathing Life into a Dead Language: Neural Architecture for Density Estimation Ofer Mell; Sebastian Ruder; Philipp Matzke; Kalyanmoy Deb; Lorenzo Soria; Maria Buiatti; Carsten Cerny Neural architecture for density estimation. One problem with neural nets is that they often “learn” to copy instead of truly learning the mapping from input to output. If this happens in the context of source code generation, this could mean that the software will only reproduce the exact input that resulted in the correct output during training. In the worst case, this could result in invalid, unstable or even malicious code. In this paper, we present a neural architecture that addresses this issue by using a variation-generator to add stochasticity to the input to the generator. This can also be viewed as a form of importance sampling that ensures that the density ratios computed by the net are well-behaved. This work builds on the RealNVP approach by @DBLP:journals/corr/abs-1808-10507 which uses a mixture of isomorphic models to approximate the true posterior distribution.

This paper uses unsupervised learning to accelerate neural network training. The idea is to train a generative model to mimic the distribution of the given training set and use the model to compute the gradients of the cost function. The difference between evaluation and train dataset can be minimized using the model to compute the gradients. This approach is called introspective training. The author compared the performance of three versions of introspection: linear introspection, zeroth order injective introspection, and first order injective introspection. They all observed significant improvement over sgd, rms, and momentum with minimal additional computation. Reviewed Element:The method is indeed effective and with minor modification it could be even more so. One issue I have is the language, there are many jargon and terms that are not explained which would be of interest to a more specialist audience. I think a more rigorous Zeroth Order Injective framework would be more valuable than the Linear variant which seems to miss some of the critical elements. Additionally I don't believe they have hit the steepest descent when they say they have minimized the approximation error, it could be a local minimum or even unstable. Overall a strong start with some necessary improvements to make it stronger. Reviewed Element:Thanks for the careful review, I have added some extra sentences to describe zeroth order injective in more details. I also explain the steepest decent calculation more clearly. Reviewed Element:As mentioned in the review, this is a strong start with some necessary improvements to make it stronger. We define and describe the problem, provide background on the method and setting, and provide the results of our implementation on CIFAR and ImageNet. We also include some additional implementation notes and a description of how the results could be improved.

The paper of offline bilingual word vectors, orthogonal transformations and the inverted softmax is an excellent work. The manuscript is well-written and the results are well-presented. I have only some minor comments. First, the "orthogonal" mapping they perform is in fact the Singular Value Decomposition, which is an orthogonal transformation. Second, in order to reduce the error caused by the off-diagonal entries in the corpus similarity matrix, the authors conduct a fine-tuning procedure. However, the authors do not describe in detail how the fine-tuning process works. How many times of fine-tuning is needed? How much do the results improve after each time of fine-tuning? These are some questions that the authors should address in the future work. Overall, this is a nice and useful paper that clearly shows how to apply orthogonal transformations to word vectors, and it also develops a relatively simple but very effective method to reduce the error caused by the off-diagonal entries in the corpus similarity matrix. I highly recommend this paper to publish in the top conferences like NAACL HUPAP or EMNLP. I wish this paper can be published in a top conference soon. Best regards! The paper Fast Chirplet Transform to Enhance CNN Machine Listening - Validation on Animal calls and Speech provides a method to use chirplet transform to enhance speech and animal calls for machine listening. The proposed technique first partitions the non-stationary nature of the sounds into linear frequency modulated (LFM) components. It then applies chirplet transform to these components to further mitigate their non-linear nature. The enhancements produced by the proposed technique were validated on Animal calls and Speech datasets. The results show that the improvements are statistically significant. The proposed technique first partitions the non-stationary nature of the sounds into linear frequency modulated (LFM) components. It then applies chirplet transform to these components to further mitigate their non-linear nature. The enhancements produced by the proposed technique were validated on Animal calls and Speech datasets. The results show that the improvements are statistically significant. The major strength of the proposed technique is that it can be applied to both animal calls and speech, because they share similar non-stationary signal properties. The technique only requires one set of parameters to work across both domains. However, the technique also has limitations. First, it assumes that the raw input sound is already of adequate length for the chirplet transform to be applied, which is not always the case. The technique also assumes that the chirplet transform parameters are knowable a priori, which may not be the case. Overall, the proposed technique provides a useful starting point for applying non-stationary signal processing techniques to enhance animal calls and speech for machine listening. In the classical machine learning, regression is treated as a black box process of identifying a suitable function from a hypothesis set without attempting to gain insight into the mechanism connecting inputs and outputs. In the natural sciences, however, finding an interpretable function for a phenomenon is the prime goal as it allows to understand and generalize results. This paper proposes a novel type of function learning network, called equation learner (EQL), that can learn analytical expressions and is able to extrapolate to unseen domains. It is implemented as an end-to-end differentiable feed-forward network and allows for efficient gradient based training. Due to sparsity regularization concise interpretable expressions can be obtained. Often the true underlying source expression is identified. In this paper authors use an innovative technique called “dual coordinate descent” to optimize the underlying optimization problem of finding the true underlying source expression. The first part of the algorithm is to train a model on a labeled data set and generate guesses for the learnable parameters. This guess is called an initialization. Then, the proposed algorithm first trains the model by a standard backpropagation algorithm to find the minimum of the objective function and then use dual coordinate descent to further optimize the objective function with respect to the parameters which have constant values, i.e., biases and kernel weights. The main contribution of this work is the new technique called dual coordinate descent that allows to find both the model parameters with constant values and the values of variables that are functions of the model parameters. The rest of the paper is organized as follows. The basic concepts of feed-forward neural networks are presented in Section II. In Section III, the proposed EQL model is introduced. In Section IV, the training algorithm is described and some experiments are presented. In Section V, we summarize the main contributions of this work.

All the ideas and implementation in the paper are very interesting. And the conclusion is reasonable. The results on language modeling and image generative modeling tasks are also very interesting. However, there are some typos in the paper. For example, Line 15 in page 5, "the mean and variance between two consecutive time steps" should be "the mean and variance of two consecutive time steps".
